The main technical challenges that present themselve

the drive for greater sensitivity and the adverse eﬀect
h
of propagation through the interstellar medium (ISM)

the observed signal (Hankins & Rickett 1975). Phase
a
coherent dedispersion is applied directly to the volt
age signal, which must be sampled at the Nyquist rat
and either recorded or reduced using a high-speed dig
ital signal processing system. Owing to the relativel
high demand on both data storage and computing re
sources, the earliest baseband recording and processin
systems were limited to relatively small bandwidths
For example, coherent dedispersion was pioneered us
ing a baseband recorder with a modest bandwidth o
125 kHz, which could record with a 20% duty cycl
for only 3 minutes before ﬁlling the magnetic tap
(Hankins 1971).
Fortunately,  the capacity of baseband recordin
and processing systems closely follows the continua

1

The dspsr software processes continuous streams o
radio pulsar astronomical data, producing integrate
statistics such the phase-resolved average polarisatio
of the pulsar signal. The salient features of this soft
ware are
automatic excision of invalid data, such as those los
•
during transfer or corrupted by impulsive interference
correction of digitisation distortions via dynamic out
•
put level setting (Jenet & Anderson 1998);

phase-coherent dispersion removal (Hankins & Rickett
•
o  1975) with optional Jones matrix convolution for high
s  time-resolution polarimetry (van Straten 2002);
e  synthetic ﬁlterbank formation with concurrent co-
•
-  herent dedispersion and/or Jones matrix convolution;
ple,  full-polarimetric detection;
•
e  computation of phase-resolved averages (folding) us-
•
-  ing either a polynomial approximation to the pulsar
a  phase model, a constant period, or acceleration search
r  parameters;
-  formation of sub-integrations of arbitrary length, in-
•
d  cluding single pulses;
,  simultaneous folding of multiple pulsars, such as glob-
•
a  ular cluster or double pulsars;
o  exploitation of multiple processing cores using par-
•
).  allel computing threads;
-  accelerated computation on graphics processing units;
•
d  transparent buﬀering of  input data to handle the
•
edge eﬀects of certain algorithms, such as the overlap-
-  save method of discrete convolution;
ly  real-time signal processing directly from a ring buﬀer
•
-  in shared memory; and
-  time-division multiplexing:  discontiguous segments
•
f  of data may be processed as a single stream to min-
n  imise initialisation time.
.  The following sections describe these features in
s  greater detail while outlining a typical pulsar signal
-  processing path.
-
2.1  Invalid Data Excision
-
-  Any operation may ﬂag sections of data as invalid, such
g  that they are ignored by subsequent components in the
e  signal processing chain. This design feature is imple-
-  mented as an array of weights that is maintained in
n  parallel with the blocks of data that represent the as-
h  tronomical signal. The weights array is typically used
to ﬂag segments of data in the time domain, such that
,  a single weight applies to all frequency channels and
-  polarisations over some epoch. However, the weights
-  array can also be used to ﬂag one or more speciﬁc fre-
s  quency channels as invalid.
-  Many of the classes used to convert between n-
g  bit and ﬂoating point representations of the digitised
g  signal monitor the data and ﬂag sections as invalid
-  whenever certain statistics (such as the noise power)
g  fall outside the acceptable ranges of operation.  The
weights array is used by operations that perform in-
tegrations (such as the pulse proﬁle folding operation
described in Section 2.6) to ensure that ﬁnal results are
not corrupted by invalid data.  For example,  invalid
data may be recorded before the instrumentation is
f
properly initialised. As illustrated in Figure 1, strong
d
bursts of impulsive interference such as lightning are
n
completely excised using this technique.
-
t  2.2  Dynamic Output Level Setting
;
Analog-to-digital conversion is a non-linear process that
-
introduces both noise and signal distortion. Dynamic
output level setting aims to correct the quantisation
distortion by restoring the linear relationship between
digitised and undigitised power (Jenet & Anderson 1998,

2.4  Synthetic Filterbank
Though phase-coherent dispersion removal retains th
original temporal resolution of the digitised signal, thi
resolution is often integrated away when forming th
average pulse proﬁle. It therefore proves useful to trad
some time resolution for frequency resolution by divid
ing the observed band into a number of sub-bands
or channels.  Both the smearing time,  td,  and th
sampling rate, r, in each sub-band is reduced, result
ing in smaller nd; this reduces the transform length
N, required for subsequent coherent dedispersion, an
thereby improves computational eﬃciency.  Further
more, with greater frequency resolution the measure
polarisation may be better calibrated, or individua
sub-bands corrupted by narrow-band radio-frequenc
interference (RFI) may be deleted. Finally, when fre
quency resolution is retained, a better estimate of th
dispersion measure may be applied at a later time t
correct the inter-channel dispersion delays before fur
ther integrating in frequency.  Using dspsr, the ob
served signal may be divided into Nc frequency chan
nels using either the deprecated or convolving ﬁlter
bank, both of which are based on the FFT.

-  2.4.1  Deprecated Filterbank
o
In the deprecated ﬁlterbank, the data are divided into
non-overlapping segments of Nc complex points (or
2Nc real points). Each segment is transformed into the
if
Fourier domain, where each of the Nc complex spectral
t
values is treated as a time sample in one of Nc inde-
s  ′
pendent signals, each with bandwidth, δν = δν/Nc,
n  ′
and sampling rate, r = r/Nc. Phase-coherent disper-
sion removal is then separately performed on each of
the Nc resulting channels using unique phase disper-
sion frequency response functions, each tuned to the
centre frequencies of the output ﬁlterbank channels.
This method of synthetic ﬁlterbank formation is
equivalent to the coherent ﬁlterbank described in Sec-
e
tion 3.1.3 of Jenet et al. (1997). Although it reduces
s
the computational cost of coherent dedispersion (see
e
the following section and the Appendix for further dis-
e
cussion), the technique suﬀers from the spectral leak-
-
age of the discrete Fourier transform, which mixes a
,
signiﬁcant amount of power between neighbouring fre-
e
quency channels (NR, Section 13.4). When the inter-
-
channel dispersion delay is large, the artifacts of spec-
,
tral leakage are readily observed as delayed images of
d
the pulse proﬁle, as shown in Figure 4.
-

l  channels of the deprecated ﬁlterbank (which ranges
y  from  88 to 154 µs across the band) is seen between
∼
-  the peaks (main and inter-pulse) and the leakage arti-
e  facts on either side of them.

The remaining artifacts in
o  the APSR convolving ﬁlterbank are introduced by the
-  Parkes Digital Filterbank (PDFB3), which is used to

-  16MHz bands. The two-stage (1024-channel analysis
-  followed by 16  64-channel synthesis) polyphase ﬁlter-
×
bank implemented by the PDFB3 introduces spectral
leakage between the 250 kHz channels produced by the
analysis ﬁlterbank; the dispersion delay between these
channels ranges from  44 to 77µs across the band.

2.5  Detection
If the voltage signals from both receptors of a dual
polarisation receiver are available, dspsr computes th
polarisation of the electromagnetic radiation.  Radi
pulsar polarimetry yields information about the physic
of the emission mechanism (e.g. Edwards & Stapper
2004), the geometry of the pulsar’s magnetosphere (e.g
Johnston et al. 2005), and the average magnetic ﬁel
of the ISM along the line of sight (e.g. Han et al. 2006)
It also provides additional constraints that may be ex
ploited for high-precision pulsar timing (van Strate
2006).
Polarisation is described by the second-order statis
tics of the transverse electric ﬁeld vector e, as give
†
by the complex 2  2 coherency matrix, ρ  e e
×  ≡ h
(Born & Wolf 1980). Here, the angular brackets de
†
note an ensemble average, e  is the Hermitian trans
pose of e, and an outer product  is  implied by th
treatment of e as a column vector.  A pulse phase
resolved ensemble average is performed by the foldin
operation described in the next section. When detect
ing synthetic ﬁlterbank data, the coherency matrix i
computed independently in each frequency channel.
A useful geometric relationship between the com
plex two-dimensional space of the coherency matri
and the real four-dimensional space of the Stokes pa
rameters is expressed by the following pair of equa
tions:
ρ  =  Sk σk/2  (8
Sk  =  tr(σkρ).  (9
Here, Sk are the four Stokes parameters, Einstein no
tation is used to imply a sum over repeated indeces
0  k  3, σ0 is the 2  2 identity matrix, σ1−3 ar
≤  ≤  ×
the Pauli matrices, and tr is the matrix trace operator

7
-  The Stokes four-vector is composed of the total inten-
sity S0 and the polarisation vector, S = (S1, S2, S3).
Equation (8) expresses the coherency matrix as a linear
combination of Hermitian basis matrices; equation (9)
p
represents the Stokes parameters as the projections of
the coherency matrix onto the basis matrices.
,  The fourth-order moments of the electric ﬁeld, as
g  described by four-dimensional covariance matrix of the
Stokes parameters, can optionally be formed and aver-
aged as a function of pulse phase. The covariances of
the Stokes parameters provide additional information
ue  about the pulsar radiation, such as the presence of or-
thogonally polarised modes of emission and the degree
of correlation between mode intensities (McKinnon & Stinebring
1998; van Straten 2009).
e
2.6  Folding
Ensemble-averages of the detected signal are computed
-
as a function of topocentric pulse phase during a pro-
c
cess commonly called folding.  Apparent topocentric
-
pulse phase is determined using a polynomial approx-
n
imation to the pulsar timing model,  φ(t), generated
s  P  7
using either the tempo (Taylor & Weisberg 1989)  or
d  8
tempo2 (Hobbs et al. 2006)  software packages. The
detected signal of interest, Y (ti), is folded by integrat-
ing each of  its samples into one of n equally spaced
phase bins, Y (φk), where bin number, k, is given by
-  k = (n φ(ti))mod n,  (10)
e  P
o  where  φ has dimensionless phase (turns) and the mod
P
s  operator returns the remainder after division, such that
s  0  k < n.
≤
.  Typically, a vector of processes, Y (ti) is folded in
d  parallel with one count, N(φk), of the number of time-
.  samples accumulated in each phase bin. For example,
-  Y (ti) may represent the four Stokes parameters in each
n  of the Nc frequency channels. The average pulse proﬁle
of each process is then given by
-
Yj(φk) = Yj(φk)/N(φk).  (11)
n  h  i
The start and end times, t0 and tN, of the observation
i
-  are used to assign the mean time of the rising edge of
-  phase bin zero. Under the assumption that  φ and its
P
e  inverse may be calculated with suﬃciently high accu-
-  racy, this is given by
g  −1
τ0 =  φ  (  φ((t0 + tN)/2) mod 1)  (12)
-
P  P
s  dspsr
During the folding operation,  can divide the
signal into segments with arbitrary start and end times
-
and produce a unique set of pulse proﬁles for each seg-
x
ment. This feature can be used to divide a long obser-
-
vation into regular intervals, or sub-integrations, that
-
may be later combined using an updated version of
the pulsar timing model, or following the deletion of
sub-integrations that have been corrupted by instru-
)
mentation faults or radio frequency interference.
)  The start and end times of each sub-integration can
also be made to coincide (within the temporal resolu-
-
tion of the signal) with integer values of pulse phase,

Digital  signal processing is demanding of computa-
h
tional, data communications, and data storage resources.
n
Therefore,  the performance of a baseband recording
,
and processing system depends on a number of vari-
d
ables, including the number of microprocessors, their
o
architecture and clock speed, memory size and band-
s
width,  cache size and bandwidth,  compiler version,
.
and optimisation options.  To demonstrate some of
d
these basic considerations, a number of illustrative bench-
e
marks are presented using currently available micro-
r
processor technology.

Overall performance is evaluated by measuring the
time required to completely process a block of data
normalised by the real time spanned by that block of
data (the real time is equal to the sampling interval
times the number of samples in the block). Figure 6
plots the processing time to real time ratio as a func-
tion of dispersion measure for four diﬀerent instrumen-
tal conﬁgurations.  For each conﬁguration,  the pro-
cessing time is that required to convert 8-bit digitised
data to single-precision ﬂoating-point complex num-
bers, form a ﬁlterbank with 500 kHz resolution while
performing phase-coherent dispersion removal, detect
the polarisation of the signal, and fold all  frequency
channels and polarisation parameters with a resolution
s
of 1024 phase bins.

The relatively modest bandwidths used for the per-
2  formance benchmarks presented in Figures 6 and 7 are
h  roughly an order of magnitude smaller than currently
-  available with modern pulsar instrumentation. To pro-
k  cess larger bandwidths in real time, the digitised sig-
d  nal must be divided between multiple workstations,
t  the number of which depends upon the method of di-
-  vision. Using time-division demultiplexing, each ma-
chine must process the full bandwidth for some fraction
e  of the time. Either the frame size must be relatively
,  large or the frames must overlap in time to compensate
e  for the data lost to edge eﬀects, which may necessitate
r  the use of one or more intermediate workstations to
d  buﬀer the frames. Using frequency-division demulti-
r  plexing, each machine must process some fraction of

dspsr is a high-performance, general-purpose tool for
radio astronomical data reduction that has enabled a
diverse range of experiments, including the discovery
of the giant micropulse phenomenon (Johnston et al.
2001),asurveyforsub-millisecondpulsars(Edwards et al.
2001),high-precision pulsartiming(van Straten et al.
2001;Hotan et al.2004a;Jacoby et al.2005;Ord et al.
